summary: This advice column tackles four distinct questions: retirement savings policy, career transitions into data science, campus safety for college-bound daughters, and sexism in STEM fields. O'Neil argues that 401(k) plans are fundamentally flawed because they only work for the wealthy, advocating instead for expanded Social Security. On campus safety, she draws from her Berkeley experience during the Gulf War protests to argue that colleges are not the safe havens their marketing suggests, and parents should prepare daughters with practical safety strategies. Her advice to women in STEM is to focus on personal passions rather than external perceptions of being 'a woman in tech.'
key_points: 401(k) plans are savings strategies that only work for well-off people, expanding Wall Street's power while missing the point of helping the middle class
College marketing creates a false sense of safety; students need to be taught self-defense and situational awareness rather than being told not to party
Parents should let teenagers experience drinking at home before college so they learn their limits in a safe environment
Women in STEM should focus on internal motivations and passions rather than external perceptions, celebrating technical victories while ignoring sexist defeats
Campus police forces are inadequate and have conflicts of interest when handling sexual assault cases
money_quotes: In this day and age, working people need all their money, and 401(k) plans have proven to be saving strategies which are only realistic for well-off people, which entirely misses the point of how to deal with the older middle class in our country.

College administrations have come a long way on marketing their campuses as attractive and safe, but it's just a marketing thing, and it sends confusing and deeply mixed messages to parents and kids, which pisses me off.
At the end of the day, when you go to college, you are an adult, and you need to be responsible for your safety.
The most educational night of my life was a night in the summer before college, when my dad got me and my friend Becky puking drunk. I never let that happen again.
My motto is, celebrate the victories and ignore the defeats.
Don't think about yourself or your actions as A Woman In STEM. Instead, think about what your personal goals are, and what interests you, and what you'd like to learn about or accomplish.
Do it because it's cool and you love it, not because some assholes do or do not have an agenda for you.
key_insight: Whether discussing retirement policy, campus safety, or workplace sexism, the common thread is that institutions often fail individuals through marketing over substance, and people must develop practical strategies for self-reliance rather than trusting systems to protect them.
